coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of defect-free thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes for reverse osmosis desalination
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Die Ling
Zhao, Qipeng
Chung, Tai-Shung
coredata.dc:description: The rise of porous nanomaterials has stimulated a new research thrust on novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) reverse osmosis (RO) membranes. While encouraging results of greater permeability have been reported, they are usually companied by an inferior membrane selectivity due to the agglomeration of nanomaterials and low compatibility between the nanomaterials and polyamide matrix. Herein, to sustain the membrane selectivity at the molecular level, a type of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), namely UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles, have been modified with different amount of tannic acid (TA) before participating in interfacial polymerization. Due to the inherent porous nature and improved hydrophilicity of the modified MOFs, the resultant TFN membranes (referred to as TFN-TU) show a remarkable increase in water permeance compared to both conventional thin-film composite (TFC) and UiO-66-NH2 incorporated membranes (TFN-U). Thanks to the bridging effects of TA, TFN-TU membranes exhibit a higher salt rejection than TFN-U membranes for brackish water desalination. In addition, for seawater desalination, TFN-TU membranes possess a higher water flux as well as a higher salt rejection than the TFC membranes. This work may offer insights and strategies for the development of advanced TFN membranes for desalination with a high water product quality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin-film nanocomposite (TFN)
Desalination
Tannic acid (TA)
Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is funded by PUB, Singapore's National Water Agency, under the project of ?Molecular Design of Thin Film Nanocomposite (TFN) Membranes for Brackish Water and Seawater Desalination? (R279-000-583-490). We would also like to thank Dr. G. F. Sun and Aquaporine Co. Ltd. for providing the Psf substrate for this work.

This work is funded by PUB , Singapore's National Water Agency, under the project of “Molecular Design of Thin Film Nanocomposite (TFN) Membranes for Brackish Water and Seawater Desalination” ( R279-000-583-490 ). We would also like to thank Dr. G. F. Sun and Aquaporine Co., Ltd. for providing the Psf substrate for this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphic illustration of fabrication of TFN-U and TFN-TU.
FESEM and TEM images (inserted with particle size distribution chart) of (a, c) UiO-66-NH2 and (b, d) TU10.
FT-IR of TA, UiO-66-NH2, TU2, TU6, TU10 and TU14 nanoparticles.
(a) XRD spectra and (b) TGA of pristine UiO-66-NH2 and TA modified ones.

FESEM images of the top surfaces of (a) pristine substrate, substrate after spraying coating (b) UiO-66-NH2 and (c) TU10, followed by MPD immersion and rubber roller; FESEM images of the top surfaces and cross-sections of (d, g) TFC and TFN membranes with (e, h) UiO-66-NH2 and (f, i) TU10.
AFM topography images of the TFC and TFN membranes.
Contact angles of the TFC and TFN membranes.
(a) Pure water permeances of membranes under 20 and 50 bar; (b) BWRO performances of membranes under 20 bar.

SWRO performances of (a) the TFC and TFN-TU10 membranes using a 32,000 ppm NaCl solution as the feed for 8 h; (b) the TFN-TU10 membrane using an ultrafiltration permeate of natural seawater as the feed for 12 days.
Summary of surface roughness and surface area of membranes by AFM.
Comparisons of desalination performances in this work and recent works on TFN RO membranes incorporated with porous nanomaterials.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: (A) means the nanoparticles are in the water phase, while (O) means in the organic phase.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The left numbers are the BWRO performances of the control membranes, while the right ones are the performances corresponding to the optimal loadings.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of defect-free thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes for reverse osmosis desalination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore, 117585, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117585

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rise of porous nanomaterials has stimulated a new research thrust on novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) reverse osmosis (RO) membranes. While encouraging results of greater permeability have been reported, they are usually companied by an inferior membrane selectivity due to the agglomeration of nanomaterials and low compatibility between the nanomaterials and polyamide matrix. Herein, to sustain the membrane selectivity at the molecular level, a type of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), namely UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles, have been modified with different amount of tannic acid (TA) before participating in interfacial polymerization. Due to the inherent porous nature and improved hydrophilicity of the modified MOFs, the resultant TFN membranes (referred to as TFN-TU) show a remarkable increase in water permeance compared to both conventional thin-film composite (TFC) and UiO-66-NH2 incorporated membranes (TFN-U). Thanks to the bridging effects of TA, TFN-TU membranes exhibit a higher salt rejection than TFN-U membranes for brackish water desalination. In addition, for seawater desalination, TFN-TU membranes possess a higher water flux as well as a higher salt rejection than the TFC membranes. This work may offer insights and strategies for the development of advanced TFN membranes for desalination with a high water product quality.

ce:section.ce:para: Given the significance of supplying clean water for humanity's well-being and economics, vigorous research has risen for augmenting water supplies through membrane technologies [1–3]. After several decades of R&D, reverse osmosis (RO) has become the dominated technology to boost fresh water supply and water purification around the world owing to its well-approved process-simplicity and high energy-efficiency [4–6]. Currently, thin-film composite (TFC) RO membranes are formed by interfacial polymerization on a porous polymeric substrate, in which amine monomers in a water solution diffuse into an organic phase and react vigorously with the acyl chloride at the water/oil interface [7–9]. They are featured by high water permeance, salt rejection and tolerance to pressure and pH change [10,11]. Although the TFC based RO process is a mature technology, the advances in TFC RO membranes (on both support and selective layers) continue to attract global research interest nowadays because of their substantial use for various desalination purposes [12–20].

Recently, the rapid development in porous nanomaterials has catalyzed the research on thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes with the aim of overcoming the trade-off relationship between permeability and selectivity in conventional TFC membranes [21–26]. Among them, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which consist of inorganic metal centers and organic linkers, are emerging as promising nanofillers in the selective layer due to high porosity and surface area, controllable and well-defined pore size, versatile surface chemistry, and plausible compatibility with polymeric materials [27–29]. Thus, encouraging results with remarkable enhancements in water permeance have been demonstrated by introducing MOFs in the polyamide matrix [30–34].

However, several challenges must be tackled before these novel TFN membranes meet the industrial success. One technical challenge is the prevention of leaching these nano-sized particles from the membranes to the permeate, which may pose threats to the environmental safety and human health [22,35,36]. This necessitates strong chemical bonding between the nanoparticles and the polyamide matrix [37,38]. In addition, aggregation of nanoparticles during membrane fabrication is usually inevitable, resulting in an enhancement in water permeance while jeopardizing membrane selectivity [29]. Furthermore, the partial inorganic nature of MOFs would introduce non-selective voids in the polyamide matrix, resulting in an improvement in water flux at the cost of membrane selectivity [33]. However, recent studies showed that membranes with a higher permeability and a satisfactory selectivity are more important to ensure higher separation efficiency, better water quality, and minimal costly post-treatment steps [39].

Therefore, we aim to develop TFN RO membranes exhibiting both supreme permeability and selectivity for desalination. Inspired by previous works, we have modified our UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles with tannic acid (TA) which can simultaneously react with UiO-66-NH2, MPD and TMC to perform the bridging effects between MOFs and the polyamide matrix (Fig. S1) [30,33]. UiO-66-NH2 are stable in water so that the separation performance of the resultant membranes can be sustainable along the RO process [33]. In addition, UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles have intrinsic pores of 5.1 Å which enable to discriminate water molecules (2.76 Å) from larger hydrated ions of Na+ (7.16 Å) and Cl− (6.64 Å). TA is a type of polyphenol widely present in plants [40]. It is reactive to amino groups on UiO-66-NH2 and MPD via Michael addition/Schiff base reactions [30,41]. In this paper, we also modified the UiO-66-NH2 by TA solutions with different concentrations, resulting in different TA loading on the nanoparticles. The success of TA modification and different amount of TA on the nanoparticles have been proved by FT-IR and TGA in the later section. Before interfacial polymerization, TA modified nanoparticles were pre-deposited on the substrate surface by spray coating. It has also been demonstrated in previous works that TA can react with the acyl chloride groups of TMC to form ester groups during the interfacial polymerization process [42]. The effects of TA and its loading on UiO-66-NH2 on the membrane morphologies and desalination performances were investigated. The resultant TFN membranes showed a remarkable rise in water permeance compared to the control membranes while well maintaining the salt rejection in brackish water and seawater desalination. Among them, TFN membranes incorporated with 1.0 g/L TA solution modified UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles exhibited the highest water permeance. This study may render useful strategies for fabricating defect-free TFN membranes owing to its success in promoting the compatibility between the nanomaterials and polyamide matrix at a molecular level.

All chemicals and solvents, including acetic acid (AA, >99%, Merck), zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, >99.5%, Sigma-Aldrich), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich), tannic acid (TA, from grass, Sigma-Aldrich), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR grade, Fisher), m-pheylenediamine (MPD, ≥98%, TCI), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, ≥99%, Sigma-Aldrich), trimesoyl chloride (TMC, >98%, Alfa Aesar), n-hexanes (AR grade, Fisher), and sodium chloride (NaCl, Sigma-Aldrich) were used as received. Polysulfone (Psf) substrates were kindly supplied by Aquaporine Ltd. (Singapore). Deionized (DI) and ultrapure water used for material syntheses and desalination tests in this work was produced with a Sartorius system (Arium).

UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles were synthesized by the solvothermal process reported in our previous papers [33,43]. For the TA modification, a 20 mL TA solution of 0.2, 0.6, 1.0 or 1.4 g/L was added into each 20 mL aqueous solution containing 0.08 wt% UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles, and allowed sonication for 30 min. The resultant nanoparticles were washed twice by DI water and collected via centrifugation again at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and named as TU2, TU6, TU10 and TU14, respectively.

Pre-deposition of the nanoparticles on Psf substrates was performed before interfacial polymerization. They were firstly dispersed in ethanol (0.04 wt%) and sonicated for 30 min to achieve uniform dispersion. Afterwards, 7 mL of each solution containing nanoparticles were connected to the spraying nozzle (PS-275, Creos Japan) and sprayed onto a piece of Psf substrates (5 × 7 cm2) which was 15 cm below the spraying nozzle at a pressure of 1.5 bar (Fig. 1 ).

Interfacial polymerization was then carried out on the pristine and nanoparticle-deposited Psf substrates as follows. The substrate was firstly soaked in a 2 wt% MPD and 0.15 wt% SDS aqueous solution for 2 min. Then the excess solution on the substrate was removed by a rubber roller until no liquid droplets were left on the surface. The substrate was subsequently introduced to a hexane solution of 0.15 wt% TMC maintained at 10 °C using a water/ice bath for 1 min, since the reaction at 10 °C was proven to produce membranes with a higher water permeance [33,44] [30,40]. The resultant membranes were quickly dried in air and then further cross-linked at 80 °C for 5 min before being cooled down in air and stored in water for characterizations and tests.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7610FPlus) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) were employed to examine the morphology of UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles and TA-modified ones. Their chemical structures were revealed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Bruker Vertex 70) in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode. An X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance X-ray Power Diffractometer) was used to analyze the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns using an excitation source of Cu K-α. The weight percentage changes of different nanoparticles as a function of temperature were monitored by thermogravimetric analyses (TGA).

FESEM (JEOL JSM-7610F Plus) was also utilized to investigate the morphologies of membrane surfaces and cross-sections. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon, USA) at the tapping mode was employed to scan the surface microstructure and analyze the surface roughness and effective surface area. An area of 5 × 5 μm2 at 3 places was analyzed for each sample. The water contact angles of the top surfaces as an indicator of surface hydrophilicity were determined by a goniometer (DataPhysics Instruments, OCA25).

Desalination performances of the membranes including pure water permeance (PWP, L m−2 h−1 bar−1, abbreviated as LMH/bar), water permeance (WP, L m−2 h−1 bar−1, LMH/bar), water flux (J
w , L m−2 h−1, LMH) and salt rejection (R, %) were evaluated using a lab-scale filtration setup. All tests were performed with a membrane size of 3 × 5 cm2 at a cross-flow velocity of 2 L/min. After pre-compaction and stabilization for 30 min, permeate samples were collected for 1 h. PWP values under 20 and 50 bar were measured using DI water as the feed and calculated by the following equation:
(1)
PWP = Δ V /
A × Δ t × Δ P
where ΔV (L) refers to the volume of permeate for a time period of Δt (h), A (m2) is the membrane area, ΔP (bar) is the operating hydraulic pressure.

Brackish water reverse osmosis (BWRO) at 20 bar and seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) at 50 bar were respectively performed using 2000 ppm and 32,000 ppm NaCl solutions as the feeds. Water permeances of BWRO tests were calculated similarly to PWP except offsetting the operating pressure by the osmotic pressure of the feed. The salt rejections (R, %) were calculated by the equation below:
(2)
R =
1 −
C p
/
C f
× 100 %
where C
p and Cf (mol L−1) refer to the respective NaCl concentrations of the permeate and feed obtained by the conductivity meter (Suntex Instruments, 8–241).

The water fluxes in the SWRO tests were determined according to the Eq. (3):
(3)
J w
= Δ V /
A m
× Δ t

To be compatible with the ultra-thin polyamide layer with a thickness around 200 nm, UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles with a size of approximately 50 nm have been prepared (Fig. 2a, c). After the TA modification, there are no much difference in size and crystal structure observed from the FESEM and TEM images (Fig. 2b, d). Therefore, FT-IR was further used to verify the success of the TA modification through chemical bonding between TA and UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 3 ). For all the TA modified UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles, a new band at 1615 cm−1 appears due to the formation of –C=N bonds between UiO-66-NH2 and TA (Fig. S1). The existence of TA on the nanoparticles can also be verified by the new peak of –C=O bond at 1668 cm−1 which can be from grafted TA or formed after the reaction between TA and UiO-66-NH2 by the Schiff-base reaction [30]. The disappearance of peaks in 1649 cm−1 (absorption bands of NH) and 3188 cm−1 (absorption bands of –NH2) are due to the loss of –NH2 groups in UiO-66-NH2 after their reactions with tannic acid. XPS analysis of the nanoparticles (Fig. S2) also confirms the formation of polyaniline by Michael addition between tannic acid and UiO-66-NH2.

Fig. 4a shows well matched XRD patterns between UiO-66-NH2 and the TA modified one, this indicates negligible influence on crystal structure of UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles after the TA addition. TGA analyses were also carried out to evidence the successful TA functionalization. According to Fig. 4b, the weight loss curves of TA modified nanoparticles are between those of UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles and TA, indicating successful grafting of TA on the nanoparticle surfaces. We can also see that the weight losses increase according to the TA concentration which signifies the increasing TA amount on the nanoparticles of TU2, TU6, TU10 and TU14.

Prior to polymerization, ethanol solutions containing 0.04 wt% UiO-66-NH2, TU2, TU6, TU10 and TU14 nanoparticles were deposited on the surfaces of Psf substrates via spray-coating. Fig. 5b, c show the FESEM images of substrate surfaces after spray-coating UiO-66-NH2 and TU10, followed by soaking in a MPD solution and dried by a rubber roller. A relatively uniform dispersion of nanoparticles on the substrates has been observed.

The pristine Psf substrates and the ones with nanoparticles (UiO-66-NH2, TU2, TU6, TU10, TU14) were then subjected to interfacial polymerization. The resultant membranes are respectively designated as TFC, TFN-U, TFN-TU2, TFN-TU6, TFN-TU10 and TFN-TU14. As shown in Fig. 5d, the surface of TFC membranes has a ridge-and-valley morphology. With regards to the nanoparticles incorporated membranes, there are no obvious defects or nanoparticle clusters from the top surfaces (Fig. 5e, f), indicating that nanoparticles are fully encapsulated within the polyamide layers, which greatly alleviates the risk of leaching nanoparticles from the membranes to the permeate in the desalination process.

For TFN-TU10, a more nodular structure appears on the membrane surface rather than the ridge-and-valley morphology. This phenomenon may be attributed to two factors. (1) As illustrated in Fig. 1, more interactions have been built up between TA modified UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles and the polyamide layer so the polyamide matrix wraps the nanoparticles to form such nodular structure; (2) another possible reason is that the existence of TA modified UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles on the substrates has hindered the MPD diffusion from the substrate pores into the organic phase for the interfacial polymerization reaction. Therefore, due to the stronger connection and decelerated polymerization process, a less loose and curved polyamide matrix has been formed. From the cross-sections of membranes (Fig. 5g, h, i), more voids can be seen in the TFN-U membrane due to the addition of unmodified UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles. Although these voids can help promote the water permeance, they may also impair the membrane selectivity to some extent [33].

Fig. 7 presents the contact angles of membrane surfaces before and after the TA modification. The control TFC membranes shows a relatively hydrophobic nature with an average contact angle of 90.1°. As TA contains a lot of phenol groups, which endow higher hydrophilicity to the membrane surface after being incorporated in the polyamide matrix. Therefore, the contact angle decreases to 76.5° for TFN-TU10. However, it is elevated to 89.1° again when TU14 nanoparticles are incorporated. This might be attributed to the more compact selective layer due to the bulky polymer chains of TA, thus hindering the wettability of the membranes. In addition, overdose of TA may reduce the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface since TA-TMC exposes more hydrophobic entities because, after their reaction, a lot of hydrophobic benzene rings from TA left.

The desalination performances in terms of water permeance and selectivity were investigated with a lab-scale cross-flow filtration setup. As shown in Fig. 8a, the TFC membrane possesses a PWP of 2.64 and 2.59 LMH/bar at 20 and 50 bar, respectively. After the incorporation of the pristine and TA modified UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles, the resultant membranes show greater PWPs than the TFC membrane at both 20 and 50 bar (Fig. 8a). These improvements stem from the promoted surface hydrophilicity and the intrinsic pores (5.1 Å) of the MOF structures which create additional transport channels for water molecules (2.76 Å). For the TFN-TU14 membrane, due to a higher TA amount on the nanoparticle surface, the selectivity layer becomes more compact and the transport of water molecules across the membranes is adversely affected. Thus, a drop in PWP is observed. Among all the TFN membranes, TFN-TU10 exhibits the highest PWP of 3.61 and 3.30 LMH/bar at respective 20 and 50 bar.

Fig. 8b provides the BWRO performances of membranes at 20 bar with 2000 ppm NaCl as the feed. The water permeances of these membranes follow the same trend as the PWPs. The TFC membrane possesses a satisfactory salt rejection of 98.82%, while the rejection drops to 98.64% for the TFN-U membrane. The lower rejection has been commonly observed in TFN membranes (Table 2 ) because the low compatibility between nanoparticles and the polyamide matrix may result in non-selective voids. Since UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles have intrinsic pores of 5.1 Å which enable to discriminate water molecules (2.76 Å) from larger hydrated ions of Na+ (7.16 Å) and Cl− (6.64 Å), the jeopardy by non-selective voids on membrane selectivity is not significant. With the additional aid of TA, non-selective voids can be healed via the interactions of TA-MPD and TA-TMC. Subsequently, all TFN-TU membranes show a higher salt rejection than TFN-U. For TFN-TU10 and TFN-TU14, the salt rejections are 98.91 and 98.96% respectively.

Based on the BWRO performance, TU10 was considered as the optimal nanofillers so that it was selected for 8-hour SWRO tests with a 32,000 ppm NaCl solution acting as the simulated seawater. As shown in Fig. 9a, the TFC membrane has a flux of about 35 LMH and a salt rejection of 99.32%, while the TFN-TU10 membrane exhibits a higher water flux around 42 LMH and a higher and stable salt rejection of 99.43%. A continuous 12-day SWRO performance test of the TFN-TU10 membrane was also conducted using an ultrafiltration permeate of natural seawater collected from a Singapore desalination plant as the feed (Fig. 9b). The water flux drops slowly from 46.0 to 40.1 LMH while the salt rejection keeps stable along the process.

The TFN membranes containing TA modified UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles have been successfully fabricated in this work. Our results indicate that by addition of these nanoparticles, the new membranes show higher water permeances without the sacrifice of salt rejections simultaneously. Among them, TU10 nanoparticles show the optimal loading of TA, and the resultant TFN-TU10 membrane exhibits 3.61 and 3.30 LMH/bar PWP at 20 and 50 bar, respectively. Moreover, it has salt rejections of 98.91 and 99. 43%, respectively, for BWRO and SWRO desalination. This work may shed a new light on the fabrication of defect-free and high-performance TFN membranes in the future.
Die Ling Zhao: Methodology, Validation, Writing-Original Draft. Qipeng Zhao: Visualization, Investigation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
Tai-Shung Chung: Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by PUB, Singapore's National Water Agency, under the project of “Molecular Design of Thin Film Nanocomposite (TFN) Membranes for Brackish Water and Seawater Desalination” (R279-000-583-490). We would also like to thank Dr. G. F. Sun and Aquaporine Co., Ltd. for providing the Psf substrate for this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2021.115230.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging R&D on membranes and systems for water reuse and desalination

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potable water reuse through advanced membrane technology

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anticipating the next century of wastewater treatment
